_comment: Rerefons editorial per autor/text, injectat al context de l'LLM com a CONTEXT (no és font citable). Clau = nom canònic (igual que authors_aliases.json i el camp author: del header SIGPHI). Bios factuals i neutrals: dates, tradició, rol i obres principals. Per als textos anònims/sagrats és una nota d'origen, no una biografia.
Adam Smith: Scottish moral philosopher and political economist (1723–1790), a central figure of the Scottish Enlightenment. Author of The Theory of Moral Sentiments (1759) and The Wealth of Nations (1776).
Al-Ghazali: Persian Muslim theologian, jurist and philosopher (c. 1058–1111), associated with Sunni Ash'ari theology and Sufism. Author of The Incoherence of the Philosophers and The Revival of the Religious Sciences.
Alexander Berkman: Russian-American anarchist writer and activist (1870–1936), an associate of Emma Goldman. Author of Prison Memoirs of an Anarchist; imprisoned for the 1892 attempted assassination of the industrialist Henry Clay Frick.
Alexis de Tocqueville: French political thinker and historian (1805–1859). Author of Democracy in America (1835–40) and The Old Regime and the Revolution.
Aristotle: Greek philosopher (384–322 BC), student of Plato and founder of the Lyceum. Wrote on logic, metaphysics, ethics, politics and natural science; tutor of Alexander the Great.
Auguste Comte: French philosopher (1798–1857), founder of positivism and an early figure in sociology. Author of the Course in Positive Philosophy.
Augustine: Bishop of Hippo in Roman North Africa (354–430) and Latin Church Father. Author of the Confessions and The City of God; a major influence on Western Christian theology.
Averroes: Andalusian Muslim philosopher and jurist (Ibn Rushd, 1126–1198), known for his commentaries on Aristotle. Author of The Incoherence of the Incoherence.
Avicenna: Persian polymath, physician and philosopher (Ibn Sina, c. 980–1037). Author of The Book of Healing and The Canon of Medicine.
Boethius: Roman senator and philosopher (c. 477–524). Wrote The Consolation of Philosophy while imprisoned and transmitted Greek logic to the Latin Middle Ages.
Charles Darwin: English naturalist (1809–1882) who proposed the theory of evolution by natural selection in On the Origin of Species (1859).
Charles Peirce: American philosopher, logician and scientist (1839–1914), founder of pragmatism and a pioneer of semiotics.
Christine de Pizan: Italian-born French author (c. 1364–c. 1430) at the court of Charles VI. Wrote The Book of the City of Ladies and took part in the querelle des femmes on women's standing.
Cicero: Roman statesman, orator and philosopher (106–43 BC) who transmitted Greek philosophy in Latin. Works include On Duties, On the Republic and On the Nature of the Gods.
Confucius: Chinese teacher and philosopher (551–479 BC) of the Spring and Autumn period. His teachings, recorded by disciples in the Analects, founded the Confucian tradition.
David Hume: Scottish Enlightenment philosopher, historian and empiricist (1711–1776). Author of A Treatise of Human Nature and An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding.
Denis Diderot: French Enlightenment philosopher and writer (1713–1784), co-founder and chief editor of the Encyclopédie.
Descartes: French philosopher and mathematician (1596–1650), a central figure of rationalism. Author of the Discourse on Method and Meditations on First Philosophy.
Diogenes Laertius: Greek biographer (3rd century AD), author of Lives and Opinions of Eminent Philosophers, a principal source for ancient Greek philosophy.
Emile Durkheim: French sociologist (1858–1917), a founder of modern sociology. Author of The Division of Labour in Society, Suicide and The Elementary Forms of Religious Life.
Emma Goldman: Lithuanian-born American anarchist writer and activist (1869–1940). Author of Anarchism and Other Essays.
Empedocles: Greek pre-Socratic philosopher from Sicily (c. 494–c. 434 BC) who proposed the four classical elements. His work survives only in fragments.
Epictetus: Greek Stoic philosopher (c. 50–c. 135 AD), born enslaved. He wrote nothing himself; his teachings were recorded by his student Arrian in the Discourses and Enchiridion.
Epicurus: Greek philosopher (341–270 BC), founder of Epicureanism, which holds pleasure understood as tranquillity to be the highest good. Most of his works survive only in letters and fragments.
Erasmus: Dutch Renaissance humanist and Catholic theologian (c. 1466–1536). Author of In Praise of Folly; produced a critical edition of the Greek New Testament.
Francis Bacon: English philosopher and statesman (1561–1626), an advocate of empirical and inductive method. Author of the Novum Organum and the New Atlantis.
Friedrich Engels: German philosopher and political theorist (1820–1895), collaborator of Karl Marx. Co-author of The Communist Manifesto and author of The Condition of the Working Class in England.
George Berkeley: Anglo-Irish philosopher and bishop (1685–1753), a proponent of subjective idealism. Author of A Treatise Concerning the Principles of Human Knowledge.
Giordano Bruno: Italian Dominican friar and philosopher (1548–1600) who proposed an infinite universe and cosmic pluralism. Tried by the Roman Inquisition and burned at the stake.
Giovanni Gentile: Italian neo-Hegelian idealist philosopher (1875–1944) who developed 'actual idealism'. He was associated with the Italian Fascist regime and wrote on its behalf.
Han Fei: Chinese philosopher of the Warring States period (c. 280–233 BC), a leading theorist of Legalism. His essays are collected in the Han Feizi.
Harriet Martineau: English writer and early sociologist (1802–1876) who translated Comte. Author of Society in America and Illustrations of Political Economy.
Harriet Taylor: English philosopher and women's-rights advocate (Harriet Taylor Mill, 1807–1858), a collaborator of John Stuart Mill. Author of The Enfranchisement of Women.
Hegel: German idealist philosopher (1770–1831) who developed a dialectical system. Author of the Phenomenology of Spirit, the Science of Logic and Elements of the Philosophy of Right.
Henri Bergson: French philosopher (1859–1941) known for the concepts of duration (durée) and élan vital. Author of Creative Evolution; Nobel laureate in Literature (1927).
Heraclitus: Greek pre-Socratic philosopher from Ephesus (c. 535–c. 475 BC), known for the doctrine of flux and the logos. His thought survives only in fragments.
Hildegard of Bingen: German Benedictine abbess, composer and writer (1098–1179). Author of visionary, medical and theological works including Scivias; named a Doctor of the Church in 2012.
Jeremy Bentham: English philosopher and jurist (1748–1832), founder of modern utilitarianism. Author of An Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation.
Johann Fichte: German idealist philosopher (1762–1814) who developed a philosophy of the self (the 'I'). Author of the Wissenschaftslehre and the Addresses to the German Nation.
John Dewey: American philosopher and educational reformer (1859–1952), a leading figure of pragmatism. Author of Democracy and Education.
John Stuart Mill: English philosopher and political economist (1806–1873), utilitarian and liberal. Author of On Liberty, Utilitarianism and The Subjection of Women.
Juan Luis Vives: Valencian (Spanish) Renaissance humanist and scholar (1493–1540) who wrote on education, the mind and social welfare. Works include De anima et vita and De subventione pauperum.
Justin Martyr: Early Christian apologist and martyr (c. 100–c. 165 AD). Author of the First and Second Apologies and the Dialogue with Trypho, defending Christianity to the Roman authorities.
Kant: German Enlightenment philosopher from Königsberg (1724–1804). Author of the Critique of Pure Reason, the Critique of Practical Reason and the Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals.
Karl Marx: German philosopher, economist and political theorist (1818–1883), founder of historical materialism. Co-author of The Communist Manifesto and author of Capital.
Kierkegaard: Danish philosopher and theologian (1813–1855), regarded as a precursor of existentialism. Author of Either/Or, Fear and Trembling and The Concept of Anxiety.
Laozi: Legendary Chinese figure traditionally credited with the Tao Te Ching and regarded as a founder of Taoism. His historicity and dating (traditionally 6th century BC) are debated.
Leibniz: German polymath, philosopher and mathematician (1646–1716), co-inventor of the calculus. Author of the Monadology and the Theodicy.
Locke: English philosopher and physician (John Locke, 1632–1704), a founder of British empiricism and a key liberal theorist. Author of An Essay Concerning Human Understanding and Two Treatises of Government.
Lucretius: Roman poet and Epicurean philosopher (c. 99–c. 55 BC), author of the didactic poem De rerum natura (On the Nature of Things).
Mabinogion: Collection of medieval Welsh prose tales of anonymous authorship, drawing on earlier oral tradition. The corpus holds an English translation, not the Middle Welsh original.
Machiavelli: Florentine diplomat, historian and political theorist (1469–1527). Author of The Prince and the Discourses on Livy.
Maimonides: Sephardic Jewish philosopher, jurist and physician (1138–1204). Author of The Guide for the Perplexed and the Mishneh Torah.
Marcus Aurelius: Roman emperor and Stoic philosopher (121–180 AD). Author of the Meditations, personal reflections written in Greek.
Mary Astell: English philosopher and early feminist writer (1666–1731). Author of A Serious Proposal to the Ladies.
Mary Wollstonecraft: English writer and philosopher (1759–1797), an early advocate of women's rights. Author of A Vindication of the Rights of Woman.
Max Weber: German sociologist and political economist (1864–1920), a theorist of rationalization and bureaucracy. Author of The Protestant Ethic and the Spirit of Capitalism.
Mencius: Chinese Confucian philosopher (c. 372–c. 289 BC) who held that human nature inclines toward good. His teachings are collected in the Mengzi (Mencius).
Michel de Montaigne: French Renaissance writer (1533–1592) who popularized the essay form. Author of the Essais.
Miguel de Unamuno: Spanish (Basque) philosopher and writer (1864–1936) of the Generation of '98. Author of The Tragic Sense of Life and Saint Manuel Bueno, Martyr.
Montesquieu: French Enlightenment political philosopher (1689–1755). Author of The Spirit of the Laws, which developed the separation of powers, and the Persian Letters.
Nietzsche: German philosopher and classical philologist (1844–1900), known for his critiques of morality and religion. Author of Thus Spoke Zarathustra, Beyond Good and Evil and On the Genealogy of Morals.
Olympe de Gouges: French writer and revolutionary (1748–1793), author of the Declaration of the Rights of Woman and of the Female Citizen (1791). Executed during the Terror.
Ortega y Gasset: Spanish philosopher and essayist (1883–1955). Author of The Revolt of the Masses and Meditations on Quixote.
Parmenides: Greek pre-Socratic philosopher from Elea (c. 515–c. 450 BC) who argued for the unity and unchangeability of being. Survives in fragments of his poem On Nature.
Pascal: French mathematician, physicist and Catholic philosopher (1623–1662). Author of the Pensées and the Provincial Letters.
Pistis Sophia: Gnostic text (c. 3rd–4th century AD) of anonymous authorship, preserved in Coptic. The corpus holds an English translation.
Plato: Athenian philosopher (c. 428–348 BC), student of Socrates and founder of the Academy. Author of dialogues including the Republic, the Phaedo and the Symposium.
Plotinus: Greek-speaking philosopher of the Roman era (c. 204–270 AD), founder of Neoplatonism. His lectures were compiled by his student Porphyry as the Enneads.
Plutarch: Greek historian, biographer and essayist (c. 46–c. 120 AD). Author of the Parallel Lives and the Moralia.
Popol Vuh: Sacred narrative of the K'iche' Maya of anonymous authorship, transmitted orally before being transcribed in the 16th century. The corpus holds a translation.
Proclus: Greek Neoplatonist philosopher (412–485 AD), head of the Academy in Athens. Author of The Elements of Theology.
Ralph Waldo Emerson: American essayist and poet (1803–1882), a leading figure of Transcendentalism. Author of the essays Nature and Self-Reliance.
Ramon Llull: Majorcan (Catalan) philosopher, theologian and writer (c. 1232–1316). Author of the Ars Magna and Blaquerna; wrote in Catalan, Latin and Arabic.
Rosa Luxemburg: Polish-German Marxist theorist and revolutionary (1871–1919). Author of The Accumulation of Capital and Reform or Revolution.
Rousseau: Genevan philosopher and writer of the Enlightenment (1712–1778). Author of The Social Contract, Emile and the Confessions.
Schelling: German idealist philosopher (1775–1854) who developed a philosophy of nature and of identity. Works include the System of Transcendental Idealism.
Schopenhauer: German philosopher (1788–1860) who held the will to be the fundamental reality and drew on Indian thought. Author of The World as Will and Representation.
Seneca: Roman Stoic philosopher, statesman and dramatist (c. 4 BC–65 AD), adviser to Nero. Author of the Moral Letters to Lucilius and moral essays.
Sextus Empiricus: Greek physician and Pyrrhonist philosopher (2nd–3rd century AD), the principal source for ancient skepticism. Author of Outlines of Pyrrhonism.
Shankaracharya: Indian philosopher and theologian (Adi Shankara, traditionally 8th century AD) who consolidated Advaita Vedanta. Wrote commentaries on the Upanishads, Bhagavad Gita and Brahma Sutras.
Sigmund Freud: Austrian neurologist (1856–1939), founder of psychoanalysis. Author of The Interpretation of Dreams and Civilization and Its Discontents.
Sor Juana Ines de la Cruz: Mexican (New Spain) nun, poet and scholar (1648–1695) who defended women's right to learning in the Respuesta a Sor Filotea.
Spinoza: Dutch philosopher of Sephardic Jewish descent (1632–1677), a rationalist and pantheist. Author of the Ethics and the Theological-Political Treatise.
Sun Tzu: Chinese general and strategist traditionally credited with The Art of War (traditionally 6th–5th century BC). Authorship and dating are debated.
Teresa of Avila: Spanish Carmelite nun, mystic and reformer (1515–1582). Author of The Interior Castle and her Life; a Doctor of the Church.
Thomas Aquinas: Italian Dominican friar, theologian and philosopher (1225–1274) who synthesized Aristotle with Christian doctrine. Author of the Summa Theologica.
Thomas Hobbes: English philosopher (1588–1679) who developed a social-contract theory grounded in materialism. Author of Leviathan.
Thomas Paine: English-born political writer and revolutionary (1737–1809). Author of Common Sense, Rights of Man and The Age of Reason.
Thoreau: American essayist and naturalist (Henry David Thoreau, 1817–1862), associated with Transcendentalism. Author of Walden and Civil Disobedience.
Vladimir Lenin: Russian revolutionary and political theorist (1870–1924), leader of the Bolsheviks and the October Revolution. Author of The State and Revolution and Imperialism.
Voltaire: French Enlightenment writer and philosopher (1694–1778), an advocate of civil liberties and religious tolerance. Author of Candide and the Philosophical Dictionary.
Voltairine de Cleyre: American anarchist writer, essayist and poet (1866–1912), an advocate of feminism and free thought. Her writings are collected in Selected Works of Voltairine de Cleyre.
William James: American philosopher and psychologist (1842–1910), a founder of pragmatism. Author of The Varieties of Religious Experience and The Principles of Psychology.
William of Ockham: English Franciscan friar and philosopher (c. 1287–1347), a nominalist associated with the principle of parsimony ('Ockham's razor').
Xenophon: Greek historian and soldier (c. 430–354 BC), a follower of Socrates. Author of the Anabasis, the Memorabilia and the Cyropaedia.
Zhuangzi: Chinese Taoist philosopher of the Warring States period (c. 369–c. 286 BC). The Inner Chapters of the Zhuangzi are attributed to him.
Bible: Anthology of Jewish and Christian sacred texts composed by many authors over centuries. The corpus holds an English translation (King James Version, 1611), not the Hebrew and Greek originals.
Quran: Central religious text of Islam, believed by Muslims to be the word of God revealed to Muhammad (7th century AD) and compiled by his followers. The corpus holds an English translation.
Bhagavad Gita: Sanskrit scripture forming part of the Mahabharata; a dialogue between Arjuna and Krishna, traditionally attributed to Vyasa. The corpus holds an English verse translation.
Dhammapada: Anthology of Buddhist verses compiled by disciples, part of the Pali Canon. The corpus holds an English translation, not the Pali original.
Upanishads: Late Vedic Sanskrit texts on metaphysics and the self, of anonymous and composite authorship. The corpus holds English translations.
Patanjali: Indian author and compiler traditionally credited with the Yoga Sutras (composed c. 2nd century BC–4th century AD), the foundational text of classical Yoga.
Shijing: The Classic of Poetry, one of the Five Classics of the Confucian canon: an anthology of ancient Chinese poems of anonymous authorship, compiled c. 11th–7th century BC.
Gilgamesh: Mesopotamian epic poem (c. 2100–1200 BC) preserved on cuneiform tablets, of anonymous and composite authorship; among the oldest surviving works of literature.
Poetic Edda: Collection of Old Norse poems on mythological and heroic themes, of anonymous authorship, preserved chiefly in the 13th-century Codex Regius.
Snorri Sturluson: Icelandic historian, poet and politician (1179–1241). Author of the Prose Edda and Heimskringla.
Hesiod: Early Greek poet (c. 700 BC), author of the Theogony and Works and Days.
Baltasar Gracian: Spanish Jesuit writer and philosopher of the Baroque (1601–1658). Author of The Art of Worldly Wisdom (Oráculo manual) and El Criticón.
Bartolome de las Casas: Spanish Dominican friar and bishop (c. 1484–1566), a chronicler and defender of the indigenous peoples of the Americas. Author of A Short Account of the Destruction of the Indies.
Francisco de Vitoria: Spanish Dominican theologian and jurist of the School of Salamanca (c. 1483–1546), an early theorist of international law and the rights of peoples. Author of the Relectiones.
Mahabharata: Sanskrit epic of ancient India, traditionally attributed to Vyasa; one of the longest epic poems, of composite authorship over centuries. The corpus holds an English translation.
Adi Granth: The Guru Granth Sahib, central scripture of Sikhism: a compilation of hymns by the Sikh Gurus and other saints, regarded by Sikhs as the eternal Guru.
I Ching: The Book of Changes, an ancient Chinese divination text and one of the Five Classics, of composite and anonymous authorship with later commentarial layers.
Avesta: Collection of the sacred texts of Zoroastrianism, including the Gathas attributed to Zarathustra, composed and transmitted over many centuries.
Kautilya: Indian statesman and scholar (Chanakya, c. 4th century BC) traditionally credited with the Arthashastra, a treatise on statecraft and economics.
Ibn Khaldun: North African (Tunisian) historian and scholar (1332–1406), a pioneer of historiography and social theory. Author of the Muqaddimah.
Ignatius of Antioch: Bishop of Antioch and martyr (c. 35–c. 108 AD), one of the Apostolic Fathers. While being taken to Rome for execution he wrote seven letters to early Christian churches.
Jaime Balmes: Catalan (Spanish) priest, philosopher and apologist (1810–1848). Author of Fundamental Philosophy and Protestantism Compared with Catholicism.
Juan Donoso Cortes: Spanish political theorist and diplomat (1809–1853), a conservative Catholic thinker. Author of the Essay on Catholicism, Liberalism and Socialism.
Ludwig Wittgenstein: Austrian-British philosopher (1889–1951) who worked on logic, language and the mind. Author of the Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus and the posthumous Philosophical Investigations.
Angel Ganivet: Spanish writer and diplomat (1865–1898) associated with the Generation of '98. Author of Idearium español.
Edmund Husserl: German philosopher (1859–1938), founder of phenomenology. Author of the Logical Investigations and Ideas.
Nihongi: The Nihon Shoki (720 AD), one of the oldest chronicles of Japan, compiled by imperial command; covers mythology and early history.
Book of the Dead: Ancient Egyptian funerary texts (New Kingdom onward): collections of spells of anonymous authorship. The corpus holds an English translation (Papyrus of Ani).
Rig Veda: Oldest of the Vedas: a collection of Sanskrit hymns of the ancient Indo-Aryans, of anonymous and composite authorship, transmitted orally. The corpus holds an English translation.
Laws of Manu: The Manusmriti, a Sanskrit legal and ethical treatise of ancient India (c. 2nd century BC–3rd century AD), of attributed and anonymous authorship. The corpus holds an English translation. (See content note: contains caste- and gender-based provisions.)
Anne Conway: English philosopher (1631–1679) who developed a vitalist monism. Author of The Principles of the Most Ancient and Modern Philosophy.
Simon Bolivar: Venezuelan military and political leader of Spanish-American independence (1783–1830). Author of the Cartagena Manifesto and the Jamaica Letter.
Ramayana: Sanskrit epic of ancient India attributed to the sage Valmiki, recounting the story of Rama. The corpus holds an English translation.
Flora Tristan: French-Peruvian socialist and feminist writer (1803–1844). Author of The Workers' Union and Peregrinations of a Pariah.
Allan Kardec: French educator and writer (1804–1869), codifier of Spiritism. Author of The Spirits' Book.
Tanakh: The Hebrew Bible (Torah, Nevi'im, Ketuvim), the canonical collection of Jewish scriptures, of composite authorship over centuries.
Liezi: Chinese Taoist text attributed to Lie Yukou; in its present form likely compiled c. 4th century AD. The corpus holds an English translation.
Mahayana Sutras: Body of Mahayana Buddhist scriptures (including the Diamond and Heart Sutras) of anonymous authorship. The corpus holds English translations.
Lotus Sutra: Influential Mahayana Buddhist scripture of anonymous authorship. The corpus holds an English translation.
Jaina Sutras: Canonical scriptures of Jainism (including the Acaranga and Kalpa Sutra), of anonymous and attributed authorship. The corpus holds English translations.
Kojiki: The oldest extant chronicle of Japan (712 AD), compiled by Ō no Yasumaro; records myth and early history. The corpus holds an English translation.
Pi i Margall: Catalan (Spanish) politician and federalist thinker (1824–1901), briefly president of the First Spanish Republic. Author of Las nacionalidades.
Polycarp: Greek bishop of Smyrna and martyr (c. 69–c. 155 AD), a disciple of the apostolic generation and one of the Apostolic Fathers. His Epistle to the Philippians survives.
Benito Feijoo: Spanish Benedictine monk and essayist of the early Enlightenment (1676–1764). Author of the Teatro crítico universal and the Cartas eruditas.
Francesc Eiximenis: Catalan Franciscan friar and writer (c. 1330–1409). Author of encyclopedic moral and political works in Catalan, including Lo Crestià.
Anselm Turmeda: Majorcan (Catalan) Franciscan friar who converted to Islam (c. 1355–c. 1423). Author of the Dispute of the Donkey and the Book of Good Counsels.
Proudhon: French mutualist and the first self-described anarchist (1809–1865). Author of What Is Property?.
Max Stirner: German post-Hegelian philosopher (1806–1856), an egoist and individualist. Author of The Ego and Its Own.
Peter Kropotkin: Russian geographer and anarchist-communist theorist (1842–1921). Author of Mutual Aid and The Conquest of Bread.
Mikhail Bakunin: Russian revolutionary and anarchist theorist (1814–1876). Author of God and the State.
Bertrand Russell: British philosopher, logician and mathematician (1872–1970), co-author of Principia Mathematica. Nobel laureate in Literature (1950).
Ludwig Feuerbach: German philosopher (1804–1872), a critic of religion. Author of The Essence of Christianity.
Thomas a Kempis: German-Dutch canon regular (c. 1380–1471) traditionally credited with The Imitation of Christ.
Thomas Malthus: English cleric and political economist (1766–1834). Author of An Essay on the Principle of Population.
David Ricardo: English political economist (1772–1823) who developed the theory of comparative advantage. Author of On the Principles of Political Economy and Taxation.
Omar Khayyam: Persian polymath, mathematician and poet (1048–1131). Quatrains (Rubaiyat) are attributed to him, famous in the English version by Edward FitzGerald.
Herbert Spencer: English philosopher and sociologist (1820–1903) who applied evolutionary ideas to society and coined 'survival of the fittest'. Author of First Principles.
Rumi: Persian poet and Sufi mystic (1207–1273). Author of the Masnavi and the Divan-e Shams-e Tabrizi.
Jacob Boehme: German Christian mystic and theosopher (1575–1624). Author of Aurora and The Way to Christ.
Philo of Alexandria: Hellenistic Jewish philosopher (c. 20 BC–c. 50 AD) who combined Jewish scripture with Greek philosophy through allegorical interpretation.
William Godwin: English political philosopher (1756–1836), an early proponent of anarchism and utilitarian thought. Author of An Enquiry Concerning Political Justice.
Hadith: Reports of the sayings and actions of the Prophet Muhammad, collected and transmitted by his followers; foundational to Islamic law and practice alongside the Quran. The corpus holds English translations.
Clement I: Early Bishop of Rome (late 1st century), traditionally credited with the First Epistle of Clement to the Corinthians, one of the earliest Christian writings outside the New Testament.
Leo I: Pope from 440 (c. 400–461), known as Leo the Great. Defended Christological orthodoxy at the Council of Chalcedon (the Tome of Leo); left sermons and letters.
Gregory I: Pope from 590 (c. 540–604), known as Gregory the Great. Author of the Moralia in Job, the Dialogues and the Pastoral Rule; reformed liturgy and church administration.
Pius V: Pope from 1566 (1504–1572) who implemented the reforms of the Council of Trent. Promulgated the Roman Catechism and the Tridentine Mass.
Leo XIII: Pope from 1878 (1810–1903). Author of Rerum Novarum (1891), foundational for modern Catholic social teaching, and Aeterni Patris.
Pius IX: Pope from 1846 (1792–1878), the longest-reigning pope. Defined the dogma of the Immaculate Conception (1854) and convened the First Vatican Council.
Benedict XV: Pope from 1914 (1854–1922) who led the Church during the First World War. Author of Spiritus Paraclitus.
Innocent III: Pope from 1198 (c. 1160–1216), one of the most powerful medieval popes. As cardinal Lotario dei Conti he wrote De miseria humanae conditionis.
Pius XI: Pope from 1922 (1857–1939) who signed the Lateran Treaty (1929). Author of Casti Connubii and Quadragesimo Anno.
Martin Luther: German theologian and friar (1483–1546), initiator of the Protestant Reformation. Author of the Ninety-Five Theses and On the Freedom of a Christian. (See content note on his 1543 anti-Jewish tract.)
John Calvin: French theologian and reformer based in Geneva (1509–1564), founder of Reformed (Calvinist) theology. Author of the Institutes of the Christian Religion.
Philip Melanchthon: German theologian and reformer (1497–1560), a collaborator of Luther and the principal author of the Augsburg Confession.
Ulrich Zwingli: Swiss theologian (1484–1531), leader of the Reformation in Zürich. Died in battle at Kappel.
Juan de la Cruz: Spanish Carmelite friar, mystic and poet (1542–1591), reformer of the Carmelite order alongside Teresa of Ávila. Author of the Noche oscura del alma and the Cántico espiritual, central texts of Christian mysticism.
Georges Sorel: French social theorist and revolutionary syndicalist (1847–1922). Author of Réflexions sur la violence (1908), which argues for the energizing role of myth and the general strike in class struggle.
Bernat Metge: Catalan humanist writer and royal secretary (c. 1346–1413), secretary to kings John I and Martin I of Aragon. Author of Lo somni (The Dream, 1399), a Petrarch- and Boccaccio-inspired dialogue on the immortality of the soul, considered the founding work of Catalan Renaissance prose.
Ausiàs March: Valencian knight and lyric poet (c. 1400–1459), master of the Catalan-Valencian poetic tradition. His poems explore desire, spiritual love, and death with unusual psychological depth, mixing Provençal troubadour conventions with introspective intensity; among the greatest achievements of medieval Iberian literature.
Anselm of Canterbury: Italian-born Benedictine monk and Archbishop of Canterbury (1033–1109), known as the Father of Scholasticism. Formulated the ontological argument for God's existence in the Proslogion and the satisfaction theory of Atonement in Cur Deus Homo; a major bridge between patristic theology and high medieval philosophy.
Peter Abelard: French scholastic philosopher and theologian (1079–1142), known for his dialectical method and his tragic love affair with Héloïse d'Argenteuil. Author of Sic et Non (a collection of contradictory patristic authorities) and the autobiographical Historia Calamitatum; an early advocate of rational inquiry in theology.
Giambattista Vico: Italian philosopher of history, law, and language (1668–1744). Author of the Scienza Nuova (New Science, 1725, rev. 1744), which argued that civilizations pass through recurring cycles (ages of gods, heroes, and men) and that historical knowledge is attainable because humans made history themselves.
Thomas More: English humanist statesman and scholar (1478–1535), Lord Chancellor of England under Henry VIII and friend of Erasmus. Author of Utopia (1516), the first great work of the Western utopian tradition, and of theological writings against the Reformation. Martyred for refusing to recognize Henry VIII's supremacy over the Church; canonized in 1935.
Condorcet: French mathematician, philosopher and Enlightenment thinker (1743–1794), Marquis de Condorcet. Active in the French Revolution as a Girondin; imprisoned and died in mysterious circumstances. His posthumous Sketch for a Historical Picture of the Progress of the Human Mind (1795) outlines a theory of indefinite human progress through reason and science.
